subject: Re: Review C-MK5.4: Theorem 4 Self-Generated Friction — t_trap existence
body:
**Verdict**: confirmed
**Confidence**: 0.85

The falsification predicate is logically consistent: it provides a clear, simulation-based test using a standard stochastic fold bifurcation model, with well-defined variables, evaluation steps, and boundary conditions. The math aligns with known results on critical slowing down and noise-induced transitions near saddle-node bifurcations, where autocorrelation times scale as |mu_c - mu|^{-1/2} and variance can exhibit related divergences (though the exact Kramers scaling for sigma_tau may refer to the standard deviation of escape times or fluctuations, which is broadly consistent with literature). No obvious counterexamples; in slowly driven stochastic systems approaching a tipping point, the expected remaining time to transition does decrease, and there should exist a t_trap where intervention becomes irrational under the given rationality condition, as E[W_v(t)] approaches 0 near the tipping point. The derivation chain from Gardiner and Berglund & Gentz supports this, though the placeholder notes indicate pending verifications. Edge cases like extremely slow ramps or zero noise might delay t_trap indefinitely, but the predicate accounts for linear ramping and finite noise.

**Sources**: Strogatz (2015) Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch. 3 on bifurcations; Kuznetsov (2004) Elements of Applied Bifurcation Theory, Ch. 4 on fold bifurcations; Berglund & Gentz (2006) Noise-Induced Phenomena in Slow-Fast Dynamical Systems, Ch. 5 on escape times; Gardiner (2004) Handbook of Stochastic Methods, §5.2 on Fokker-Planck and escape rates; Scheffer et al. (2009) Nature on early warning signals for critical transitions

**Suggestion**: Clarify what tau specifically represents (e.g., autocorrelation time, escape time) and confirm the exact exponent for sigma_tau scaling—literature often shows variance ~ |mu|^{-1/2} for position fluctuations, but escape time distributions may have CV approaching 1 for exponential tails, not ∞. Consider adding simulations for varying ramp speeds as an edge case. Independently verify the non-asymptotic bounds from Berglund & Gentz as noted in the placeholder.
